channel.title: Math Learning Site – Worksheets
channel.description: Recent content in Worksheets on Math Learning Site

item.title: Factoring GCF Worksheet
item.description:
This factoring GCF worksheet helps students practice identifying the greatest common factor and rewriting expressions in factored form. The printable set includes 12 randomized questions and an answer key on page 2.

What is Factoring GCF?

Factoring GCF means finding the greatest common factor shared by all the terms in an expression and pulling it outside parentheses. This is often the first step in simplifying or factoring more complicated polynomials.

How to Factor Out the GCF

  1. Find the greatest common numerical factor in all terms.
  2. Check whether the variable part also has a common factor.
  3. Divide each term by the GCF.
  4. Write the expression as the GCF times the simplified parentheses.

Example

Factor 6x2+12x6x^2 + 12x6x2+12x

The greatest common factor is 6x6x6x.

So:

6x2+12x=6x(x+2)6x^2 + 12x = 6x(x + 2)6x2+12x=6x(x+2)

Factoring GCF Worksheet

12 questions Page 1 of 2

Solve each problem. Show your work.

Name:
Date:

Factoring GCF Worksheet Answer Key

12 questions Page 2 of 2

Check your work using the correct answers below.

FAQ

Q: Why do we factor out the GCF first?
A: Factoring out the GCF makes the remaining expression simpler and often reveals another factoring step.

Q: Can the GCF include variables?
A: Yes. If every term contains the same variable factor, it should be part of the GCF.

item.title: Factoring Trinomials Worksheet
item.description:
This factoring trinomials worksheet helps students practice breaking quadratic expressions into two binomial factors. The printable set includes 12 randomized questions and an answer key on page 2.

What is Factoring a Trinomial?

Factoring a trinomial means rewriting a three-term polynomial as a product of two simpler expressions. For many Algebra I problems, the goal is to find two numbers that multiply to the constant term and add to the middle coefficient.

How to Factor Trinomials

  1. Check whether there is a GCF first.
  2. Identify the trinomial in the form x2+bx+cx^2 + bx + cx2+bx+c or a similar quadratic expression.
  3. Find two numbers that multiply to ccc and add to bbb.
  4. Write the trinomial as a product of two binomials.

Example

Factor x2+7x+12x^2 + 7x + 12x2+7x+12

You need two numbers that multiply to 121212 and add to 777.

Those numbers are 333 and 444.

So:

x2+7x+12=(x+3)(x+4)x^2 + 7x + 12 = (x + 3)(x + 4)x2+7x+12=(x+3)(x+4)

Factoring Trinomials Worksheet

12 questions Page 1 of 2

Solve each problem. Show your work.

Name:
Date:

Factoring Trinomials Worksheet Answer Key

12 questions Page 2 of 2

Check your work using the correct answers below.

FAQ

Q: What should I do if I cannot find the right pair of numbers?
A: Double-check the signs and make sure the expression has been simplified fully before factoring.

Q: Do all trinomials factor over the integers?
A: No. Some trinomials are prime and do not factor nicely using integers.

item.title: Difference of Squares Worksheet
item.description:
This difference of squares worksheet helps students recognize and factor expressions that match the pattern a2−b2a^2 - b^2a2−b2. The printable set includes 12 randomized questions and an answer key on page 2.

What is a Difference of Squares?

A difference of squares is an expression with two perfect square terms separated by subtraction. It follows the pattern:

a2−b2=(a−b)(a+b)a^2 - b^2 = (a - b)(a + b)a2−b2=(a−b)(a+b)

Recognizing this pattern makes factoring much faster.

How to Factor a Difference of Squares

  1. Check that there are exactly two terms.
  2. Make sure both terms are perfect squares.
  3. Confirm that the operation is subtraction.
  4. Rewrite using the pattern (a−b)(a+b)(a - b)(a + b)(a−b)(a+b).

Example

Factor x2−16x^2 - 16x2−16

This is x2−42x^2 - 4^2x2−42.

So:

x2−16=(x−4)(x+4)x^2 - 16 = (x - 4)(x + 4)x2−16=(x−4)(x+4)

Difference of Squares Worksheet

12 questions Page 1 of 2

Solve each problem. Show your work.

Name:
Date:

Difference of Squares Worksheet Answer Key

12 questions Page 2 of 2

Check your work using the correct answers below.

FAQ

Q: Does a2+b2a^2 + b^2a2+b2 factor the same way?
A: No. The sum of squares does not factor the same way over the real numbers.

Q: How do I know if something is a perfect square?
A: Look for terms like x2x^2x2, 999, 16y216y^216y2, or anything that can be written as a quantity squared.

item.title: Mixed Factoring Polynomials Worksheet
item.description:
This mixed factoring polynomials worksheet combines GCF, trinomials, and difference of squares in one printable review. It includes 16 randomized questions and an answer key on page 2.

What Does This Worksheet Cover?

This worksheet is designed for mixed factoring review. Students practice deciding whether to factor out a GCF first, use trinomial factoring, or recognize a difference of squares pattern.

How to Approach Mixed Factoring Problems

  1. Look for a GCF first.
  2. Decide whether the remaining expression is a trinomial or a special pattern.
  3. Use the factoring method that best matches the expression.
  4. Check your answer by multiplying the factors back together.

Example

Factor 3x2−123x^2 - 123x2−12

First factor out the GCF:

3x2−12=3(x2−4)3x^2 - 12 = 3(x^2 - 4)3x2−12=3(x2−4)

Then factor the difference of squares:

3(x2−4)=3(x−2)(x+2)3(x^2 - 4) = 3(x - 2)(x + 2)3(x2−4)=3(x−2)(x+2)

Mixed Factoring Polynomials Worksheet

16 questions Page 1 of 2

Solve each problem. Show your work.

Name:
Date:

Mixed Factoring Polynomials Worksheet Answer Key

16 questions Page 2 of 2

Check your work using the correct answers below.

FAQ

Q: Why should I check for GCF first?
A: Many factoring problems are not completely factored until the GCF is removed first.

Q: How do I know which factoring method to use?
A: Start by identifying the structure of the expression: common factor, trinomial, or special product pattern.
